chosen_title: Husband taxed on wife's FD, gold & shares income — Clubbing of Income (Section 64)
selection_note: Candidate 3 (July 1 changes) scored highest at 81 but requires citing specific figures for six distinct policy changes — accuracy risk for a regulated firm. Candidate 7 (clubbing of income) at 78 is built on airtight, well-established statute (Section 64), is highly shareable, and fits the 'Money Myths' format perfectly. Gap is within editorial margin; accuracy wins.
caption:
Everyone's heard it: 'Just put the FD in your wife's name — she's in a lower slab.'

Here's what the Income Tax Act quietly disagrees with. Section 64 — the clubbing of income rule — says if YOU funded it, it's YOUR income. Doesn't matter whose name is on the FD, shares, or gold. The interest, dividends, and capital gains get added straight back to your taxable total.

Quick illustration: Transfer ₹5 lakh to your spouse → she earns ₹35,000 FD interest at 7% → Section 64 clubs it back to YOUR income → at the 30% slab that's ₹10,500 in tax anyway. Exactly what you'd have paid keeping the money. Zero saving, full audit risk. (Illustrative numbers.)

What actually works: investing from income she genuinely earns herself — salary, freelance, inheritance; NPS under 80CCD(1B) for an extra ₹50,000 deduction; ELSS or PPF in your own name, clean and documented.

What to do: Review your investment structure before you file — a wrong move today is a notice tomorrow.

Drop us a WhatsApp — we'll plan it right together.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

slides.label: MONEY MYTH
slides.image_prompt: A middle-aged Indian man sitting at a home dining table at dusk, staring with a furrowed brow at an open FD passbook with his wife's name on it, an income tax notice partially visible under his elbow, a glass of chai going cold beside him, warm lamplight, quiet domestic tension
slides.headline: Transferred money to your wife to save tax?

slides.label: MYTH vs FACT
slides.myth: Her name on the FD → her income → her lower tax slab. Smart planning.
slides.truth: Section 64 clubs that income straight back to you. If you funded it, you pay tax on it — no matter whose passbook it's in.

slides.label: REAL TALK
slides.headline: Her FD. Her PAN. Your tax bill.

slides.label: EXPLAINER
slides.headline: The ₹10,500 that went nowhere
slides.body: Transfer ₹5L to your spouse → she earns ₹35,000 FD interest at 7% → Section 64 adds it to YOUR taxable income → at the 30% slab, tax = ₹10,500. The same you'd have paid anyway. Risk of scrutiny added free of charge. (Illustrative numbers — not a guarantee.)

slides.label: LEGAL BUT GENIUS
slides.image_prompt: A confident young Indian woman in business casuals reviewing her own salary slip and a fresh investment form at a sunlit modern desk, pen in hand, documents neatly arranged, expression calm and self-assured — no man in the frame, sense of independent financial agency
slides.headline: Income she EARNS herself? Never clubbed.
slides.caption: Salary, freelance fees, or inherited money — her own income always stays hers.
slides.body: Salary, freelance fees, or inherited money — her own income always stays hers.

slides.label: TAX PLANNING
slides.title: What actually saves tax (legally)
points: ELSS / PPF in your own name — clean paper trail
HUF: a separate tax entity if your family qualifies
Invest from her OWN earned income — no clubbing, no drama
